paper_title: Femtosecond Laser Optoperforation and Analysis in Biological Samples

methods.description: This method describes the process for preparing Lilium hybrid cv Shiberia pollen, performing femtosecond laser optoperforation on the pollen exine, introducing foreign DNA for transformation studies, and assessing pollen viability and structural changes using microscopy and AFM.

steps.description: Mature pollens were obtained from the anthers of Lilium hybrid cv Shiberia.
steps.searchable_summary: obtain mature pollen from plant anthers

steps.description: After collection, the pollens were air-dried and stored at 4o C.
steps.searchable_summary: air dry and store pollen

steps.description: Before in vitro culture, pollens were rehydrated in deionized water under humid atmosphere for about 30 min
steps.searchable_summary: rehydrate pollen in deionized water

steps.description: and spread on the culture medium (pH 5.8) comprising of 145 mM sucrose, 1.6 mM H 3 BO 3 , 1.8 mM Ca(NO 3 ) 2 and 0.4 % phytagel (Sigma).
steps.searchable_summary: prepare and spread pollen on culture medium

steps.description: Near-infrared laser beam from amplified Ti:sapphire femtosecond laser at a repetition rate of 1 kHz (Quantronix) was focused on the pollen exine through objective lens (N.A. = 0.6) so that we could selectively puncture a wall inside an ornament.
steps.searchable_summary: focus femtosecond laser on pollen exine for optoperforation

steps.description: Petri dish containing the pollen grains was mounted on a motorized XYZ -translation stage, which is used to manipulate the sample to expose a fresh pollen grain surface at each laser pulse.
steps.searchable_summary: mount sample on motorized stage and manipulate position

steps.description: Laser fluence was attenuated by a variable neutral density filter.
steps.searchable_summary: attenuate laser fluence using neutral density filter

steps.description: All the laser exposers were conducted under a single shot configuration by using a fast mechanical shutter with a opening time constant of 0.6 ms, which is synchronized with the laser.
steps.searchable_summary: perform single-shot laser exposure using synchronized mechanical shutter

steps.description: The images obtained during the fs-laser irradiation on the pollen's surface were fed into a personal computer for further data analysis.
steps.searchable_summary: capture images during laser irradiation

steps.description: For the pollen transformation study, a foreign DNA with a construct, encoding F1TPae-γ mitochondrial targeting sequence fused to RFP; was added into the culture medium before the laser optoperforation (Lee et al., 2001).
steps.searchable_summary: add foreign DNA construct to culture medium for transformation

steps.description: After optoperforation, the viability of the operated pollen grains was checked by measuring the germination rate of pollen tube by intrusion of propidium iodide (PI).
steps.searchable_summary: check pollen viability using germination rate and propidium iodide staining

steps.description: The glass coverslips with grids marked on the surface, were used to cover over the pollens for the localization of the irradiated pollen grains.
steps.searchable_summary: use gridded coverslips for localizing irradiated pollen

steps.description: MitoTracker Green FM (Molecular probes, invitrogen) was further used to confirm the RFP expression. MitoTracker Green FM is a conventional fluorescent marker used for tracking of the mitochondria (Lovy-Wheeler et al., 2007).
steps.searchable_summary: confirm RFP expression using MitoTracker Green FM staining

steps.description: Pollen germination as well as its response to optoperforation was characterized with an optical microscope and a fluorescent microscope (Axioskop, Zeiss).
steps.searchable_summary: characterize pollen germination and response using optical and fluorescent microscopy

steps.description: In order to confirm the hole size, the intact pollen surface treated with laser beam was imaged by atomic force microscope (XE-120, PSIA) without any further treatments.
steps.searchable_summary: image laser-treated pollen surface with atomic force microscope (AFM) to confirm hole size

methods.method_name: Live Cell Plasma Membrane Optoperforation and Analysis
methods.description: This method details the procedure for perforating the plasma membrane of live cells using a femtosecond laser, simultaneously monitoring the process with AFM and CCD, and analyzing membrane motility using micro particle image velocimetry (μ-PIV).

steps.description: The perforation of the plasma membrane of live cells was performed with a femtosecond amplifier system (Integra, Quantronix) with maximum output energy of 1 mJ/pulse at 780 nm, of which the pulse width and the repetition rate is 150 fs and 1 kHz, respectively (Jeoung et al., 2005).
steps.searchable_summary: perform live cell plasma membrane perforation using femtosecond laser
inputs: femtosecond laser system (780 nm, 150 fs, 1 kHz)

steps.description: The laser beam was tightly focused on the membrane with a highnumerical-aperture objective lens (Nikon, X100, N.A = 1.3, oil immersion lens) mounted to an inverted microscope (Axiovert 200M, Zeiss).
steps.searchable_summary: focus laser beam on cell membrane using high-NA objective on inverted microscope

steps.description: All the experiments were done under singleshot configuration by using a fast electrical shutter with an opening time constant less than 0.5 ms.
steps.searchable_summary: conduct single-shot laser exposures using fast electrical shutter

steps.description: In order to eliminate any desperate loss of laser energy during these experiments, the fast electrical shutter was perfectly synchronized with the laser.
steps.searchable_summary: synchronize electrical shutter with laser pulses

steps.description: The laser energy before the objective lens was kept to be about 5 ~ 7 nJ/pulse by attenuating it with a neutral density filter.
steps.searchable_summary: attenuate laser energy to specific level using neutral density filter

steps.description: The changes in the topography and the optical images of plasma membrane induced by the ultrafast laser irradiations were simultaneously monitored with both an atomic force microscope (AFM) and a charge coupled device (CCD) detector, respectively.
steps.searchable_summary: simultaneously monitor membrane topography (AFM) and optical images (CCD) during laser irradiation

steps.description: AFM (XE-120, PSIA) was slightly modified and combined to an optical microscope.
steps.searchable_summary: integrate AFM with optical microscope

steps.description: AFM probe was carefully aligned to be aimed at same position of the focus of fs-laser beam.
steps.searchable_summary: align AFM probe to laser focus position

steps.description: Contact imaging of the AFM was performed using a silicon nitride bio-cantilever (Bio-lever, Olympus, Japan), of which nominal force constant is 0.03 nN/m.
steps.searchable_summary: perform AFM contact imaging using bio-cantilever

steps.description: The topographies have been obtained by optimising the AFM running parameters that gives the identical results to that of the two-way scanning configuration (Parpura et al., 1993& Velegol et al., 2003).
steps.searchable_summary: optimize AFM parameters for topography acquisition

steps.description: The set point for loading force was kept to be very weak between 0.65 nN and 1 nN, to minimize the damage or the attractional movement caused by attraction force of the AFM tip.
steps.searchable_summary: set weak AFM loading force
outputs: AFM system with set loading force

steps.description: The measurements of the velocity field responsible for the membrane motility after fs-laser perforation have been measured by a micro particle image velocimetry (μ-PIV) (Yahng et al., 2005).
steps.searchable_summary: measure membrane motility velocity field using micro particle image velocimetry (μ-PIV)

steps.description: The visualization of membrane movements was figured out by attaching a fluorescent polystyrene bead (diameter ~ 200 nm) on the plasma membrane of a target cell.
steps.searchable_summary: attach fluorescent beads to cell membrane for visualization

steps.description: Just after the fs-laser irradiation on the surface of plasma membrane, two successive ns-laser pulses with a time lapse of 10 ms were employed to capture the two fluorescence images.
steps.searchable_summary: capture two successive fluorescence images using ns-laser pulses after fs-laser irradiation

steps.description: Thereafter, the velocity fields of membrane motility were obtained by a cross correlation calculation with the two images.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate velocity fields from two images using cross correlation

steps.description: Neuroblastoma (Neuro2A) cells were used to investigate the dynamics of its plasma membrane and self-healing process.
steps.searchable_summary: use Neuroblastoma (Neuro2A) cells for experiments

steps.description: During the experiment, live cells were kept inside a sterile miniaturized cell chamber filled with Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Media (DMEM, Gibco BRL) containing 10 % Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS, Gibco) and 1 % penicillin/streptomycin (Gibco) under humid atmosphere at 5 % CO 2 partial pressure.
steps.searchable_summary: maintain live cells in culture medium under controlled atmosphere

methods.method_name: Retinal Blood Vessel Optoperforation and Histological Analysis
methods.description: This method describes the preparation of porcine retina tissue, femtosecond laser optoperforation of retinal blood vessels, mapping of laser lesions, tissue fixation, embedding, cryo-sectioning, staining, and histological analysis to determine the probability of damage.

steps.description: Experimental porcine eyes obtained from a local abattoir were deposited in ice cold Hanks's Balanced Salt Solution (HBSS) and transported to the laboratory on ice (4 o C) within 1 hr after the slaughter.
steps.searchable_summary: obtain and transport porcine eyes in cold HBSS

steps.description: After a precise incision of the porcine eyes behind the ora serrata, cornea and lens, the retina tissues were carefully peeled off from pigmented epithelium and put it onto the Whatmann 2 paper.
steps.searchable_summary: dissect and peel off retina tissue from porcine eye

steps.description: Before proceeding further for the fs-laser irradiations, the retina segments with size of about 3 X 5 mm 2 were prepared
steps.searchable_summary: prepare retina segments of specific size

steps.description: and to closely mimic the in vivo retinal conditions and to prevent the drying off the samples, we covered the tissues with 100 µl of vitreous humour during the experiments.
steps.searchable_summary: cover retina segments with vitreous humour to prevent drying

steps.description: The fs-laser system used in this study consists of a regenerative amplified Ti: Sapphire (λ = 810 nm) laser, delivering a 150-fs pulse at a repetition rate of 1 kHz (Quantronix, USA).
steps.searchable_summary: use femtosecond laser system (810 nm, 150 fs, 1 kHz)

steps.description: The laser beam is focused on the retina surface with an objective lens of N.A. 0.4 and the estimated laser beam diameter at the retinal surface is about 1.3 μm.
steps.searchable_summary: focus laser beam on retina surface using objective lens

steps.description: Similar to the previous setups, the retina tissues are mounted onto a motorized XYZ translation stage, which is used to manipulate the samples to expose a fresh area of tissue to each laser pulse (Fig. 13).
steps.searchable_summary: mount retina tissue on motorized stage and manipulate position

steps.description: The laser fluence used in the current work is in the range of 1.4 J/cm 2 ~ 99.4 J/cm 2 .
steps.searchable_summary: set laser fluence within a specific range

steps.description: With 13 different porcine eyeballs, 20 -25 laser treatments for each eye ball were conducted at intervals of 100 µm on the surface of retinal blood vessels.
steps.searchable_summary: perform multiple laser treatments on retinal blood vessels at defined intervals

steps.description: Blood vessels used in the experiments were primary in nature and were classified on the basis of the location and the diameter of the vessel.
steps.searchable_summary: classify blood vessels based on location and diameter

steps.description: All the experiments were conducted under a single-shot configuration by using a mechanical shutter with an opening time constant of 0.6 ms, which is synchronized with the laser, allows an accurate delivery of single pulse-irradiations into the samples.(Sidhu et al., 2009)
steps.searchable_summary: conduct single-shot laser exposures using synchronized mechanical shutter

steps.description: After optoperforation of blood vessel epithelium, the location of each laser lesion was carefully mapped.
steps.searchable_summary: map the location of laser lesions

steps.description: Just after laser treatment, the retina segments were immersed and fixed in 2% p-formaldehyde at 4 o C for one hour.
steps.searchable_summary: fix retina segments in p-formaldehyde

steps.description: In next step, the retina tissues were submersed in 30% sucrose for about one hour to retain the cell shape.
steps.searchable_summary: submerse fixed retina tissue in sucrose solution

steps.description: The fixed and retained samples were embedded in Jung's Tissue Freezing medium for an hour at room temperature and later on transferred to a refrigerator at -20 o C for 4-6 hrs.
steps.searchable_summary: embed tissue in freezing medium and freeze

steps.description: Transverse cryo-sections with a thickness of 20 µm (Microtome, LEICA) were made of the lesion area at -15 o C.
steps.searchable_summary: create transverse cryo-sections using microtome

steps.description: The slices were stained with Haemotoxylin and Eosin Red (H & E) to assay the laser lesions at the peripheral Inner Limiting Membrane (ILM : upper layer of cells covering the entire retina surface along with the blood vessels) and the blood vessels epithelium of the retina tissue.
steps.searchable_summary: stain tissue sections with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E)

steps.description: In order to determine the percent probability of damage for the blood vessels, we examined about ~1002 sampled (6 times) at various laser fluences in the range of 1.4 J/cm 2 -99.4 J/cm 2 .
steps.searchable_summary: examine stained sections at various fluences to determine damage probability
